data.title: Polymarket odds of U.S.-Iran peace deal top 50% for first time as Strait of Hormuz opens
data.description:
Odds of a near-term U.S.–Iran peace deal have crossed the 50% threshold for the first time, as the Middle Eastern country said the Strait of Hormuz is completely open for commercial vessels. 

Trump, however, said that the naval blockade on Iranian ports will remain in full force and effect.

Polymarket data showed the probability of a permanent deal being announced by April 30 rising to 54%, up 32 percentage points in recent sessions.

The move follows a steady climb through the week, punctuated by a sharp spike in the latest trading window, suggesting fresh information or positioning shifts.

Iran reopened the Strait of Hormuz after Israel and Lebanon announced a 10-day ceasefire on Thursday after the representatives of the two nations met in Washington on Tuesday.

Trading volumes have been robust, with over $12M in activity, indicating strong participation rather than a thin-market move.

The repricing implies that investors increasingly see a diplomatic breakthrough as plausible within a compressed timeline, despite historically volatile negotiations between the two sides.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Ericsson drops after Q1 revenue falls short of estimates; North America sales decline
data.description:
Ericsson (ERIC) dropped 6% during Friday morning market action after its first quarter fiscal 2026 financial results fell short of market expectations. 

First-quarter sales revenue declined 10% year over year to total $5.41B, which was less than the consensus estimate of $5.53B. Earnings per share of $0.03 were also less than the $0.11 estimate. 

Net income tumbled 79% year over year to $99M from $460M in the same quarter one year prior. The steep decline was led by a $420M restructuring charge related to a significant headcount reduction in Sweden.

Reported sales revenue in the Americas region declined 18% year over year.

"Network sales declined in North America, primarily reflecting accelerated network investments in the prior-year period, in part due to tariff uncertainty, as well as a short-term reallocation of customer spend following mobile network operator consolidation announced last year," Ericsson said. "Cloud Software and Services sales increased, driven by timing of project deliverables in core networks as well as North America managed services growth."

However, the company reported organic sales growth in its other market regions and total organic growth of 6%.

"Our Q1 results demonstrate continued resilience in a dynamic environment, with organic sales growth of 6%," said Ericsson CEO Börje Ekholm. "Our healthy gross margins and strong cash flow reflect the progress we have made in recent years, reducing reliance on geographic mix and strengthening our foundations globally."

"Our multi-year investments in building a resilient, diversified, supply chain have enabled us to deliver consistently for customers amidst geopolitical and macroeconomic uncertainties," he added. "We are facing increasing input costs, especially in semiconductors, caused in part by AI demand. Our ambition is to offset these challenges, by working closely with customers and suppliers, and through product substitution and efficiency actions."

data.title: Alcoa slides on Q1 miss as alumina weakness, disruptions weigh on results and outlook
data.description:
Alcoa (AA) down 7.6% in Friday's trading after missing estimates for Q1 adjusted earnings and reporting a 5% drop in revenues, as aluminum and alumina shipments declined.

Q1 profit fell to $425M, or $1.60/share, from $548M, or $2.07/share, in the year-earlier quarter, as aluminum shipments fell 8% Q/Q, mainly due to inventory repositioning in North America and decreased trading, and alumina shipments slumped 31%, partly due to delays in Australia that were related to the Middle East war and a cyclone.

In Q2, the company expects the conflict in Iran to contribute to an unfavorable impact of $15M on adjusted EBITDA in its alumina segment, while anticipating a favorable impact of $55M in adjusted EBITDA due to factors including inventory repositioning in Q1, higher shipments, and completion of the San Ciprián smelter restart earlier in April.

Alcoa (AA) said full-year guidance for alumina segment production and shipments remains unchanged from its prior projection of 9.7M-9.9M metric tons and 11.8M-12M metric tons, respectively.

The company also said full-year guidance for aluminum segment production and shipments remains unchanged, at 2.4M-2.6M metric tons and 2.6M-2.8M metric tons, respectively.

Alcoa's (AA) Q1 miss was driven "in part due to lighter-than-expected alumina shipments from seasonality and vessel constraints amid weather disruptions in Australia and the Middle East conflict," but its forecast looks solid amid tight supply, J.P. Morgan's Bill Peterson said.

data.title: Once Upon a Farm eligible for WIC benefits in California
data.description:
Once Upon a Farm (OFRM) is now eligible for Special Supplemental Nutrition Program for Women, Infants, and Children ((WIC)) benefits in California, making the company’s organic food and snacks available to the 1.3M individuals who qualify for WIC benefits in the state.

Once Upon a Farm (OFRM) is now WIC-authorized in 20 states.

The Special Supplemental Nutrition Program for Women, Infants, and Children provides federal grants for supplemental foods, health care referrals, and nutrition education for low-income pregnant, breastfeeding and non-breastfeeding postpartum women, and infants and children up to age five who are found to be at nutritional risk. Once Upon a Farm (OFRM) baby food bowls are the only organic, refrigerated food to be WIC-eligible.

Source: Press Release

data.title: PayPal gains amid activist investor speculation
data.description:
PayPal (PYL) rose 2.5% at least partly amid some speculation it could be a target for an activist investor.

SG Americas, Societe Generale's broker deal, may be becoming a swap broker that activists use, Gordon Hakett's Don Bilson wrote in a note on Friday, after looking at SG's 13F filing on Thursday. 

One potential activist target may be PayPal (PYPL) after the payments firm replaced its CEO in February amid disappointing earnings and guidance. There were also reports in February that PayPal has piqued the interest of some potential acquirers.

Other possible activist positions from SG'S 13F filing include Vici Properties (VICI), Host Hotels (HST) and GitLab (GTLB).

Shares of Vici ticked higher by 0.8%, Host Hotels rose 2.3% and GitLab was unchanged.

data.title: Affirm soars on top pick status; Robinhood, SoFi also climb
data.description:
Affirm Holdings (AFRM) stock surged 8.6% in Friday morning trading after Morgan Stanley named the company a top pick in the consumer finance sector.

Moreover, news that Iran has opened the Strait of Hormuz is likely buoying fintech and consumer finance stocks, as lower gasoline prices may free up cash for consumers to spend in other areas or give them more confidence to borrow.

Other fintech and consumer finance stocks on the rise include Robinhood Markets (HOOD) +6.3%, SoFi Technologies (SOFI) +5.3%,  LendingClub (LC) +5.8%, LoanDepot (LDI) +14%, and Rocket Companies (RKT) +7.5%.

Morgan Stanley analyst James Faucette named Affirm (AFRM) his “Top Pick” on the prospects of upward earnings estimate revisions, the easing of private credit fears, and ahead of its May Investor Forum.

In late March, the stock had dipped as low as $42.10 amid private credit fears and economic uncertainty as gasoline prices soared in response to the Middle East conflict.

The analyst sees a "particularly attractive setup" for Affirm (AFRM) in the next six months.

Faucette's overweight rating on Affirm (AFRM) aligns with the average SA Analyst rating of Strong Buy and the average Wall Street rating of Buy and contrasts with the SA Quant rating of Hold.

data.title: Dow rises 1,000 points to 2% as Strait of Hormuz opens
data.description:
The Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJI) has soared 1,000 points, or 2%, to about 49,600 as Iran declared the Strait of Hormuz open for commercial ships amid the ongoing ceasefire deal.

The blue-chip index is now 3.3% year-to-date and has added 4.4% in the past five days.

Iran announced the Strait of Hormuz is now “completely open” for all commercial vessels for the rest of the Lebanon ceasefire timeline, after Israel and Lebanon announced a 10-day ceasefire on Thursday.

U.S. President Donald Trump, however, said that the naval blockade on Iranian ports will remain in full force and effect. The reopening of the passageway was one of the conditions of the fragile two-week ceasefire that is currently in effect.

Crude oil futures (CL1:COM) sank on Friday to $81, while Brent (CO1:COM) also dropped to $86 per barrel.

Here are the top 10 stocks aiding the rise of the Dow:

  1. Sherwin-Williams (SHW) +5%
  2. Boeing (BA) +4.3%
  3. Home Depot (HD) +4.2%
  4. 3M (MMM) +3.6%
  5. American Express (AXP) +3.5%
  6. Caterpillar (CAT) +2.9%
  7. Honeywell International (HON) +2.8%
  8. Salesforce (CRM) +2.8%
  9. Goldman Sachs (GS) +2.8%
  10. Procter & Gamble (PG) +2.8%

Dow ETFs: (DIA), (DDM), (UDOW), (DOG), (DXD), and (SDOW).

data.title: Fed leadership transitions historically push yields higher - BofA
data.description:
Treasury investors may want to brace for volatility when a new Federal Reserve chair takes the helm, according to historical data compiled by Bank of America. Jerome Powell's term ends next month.

The analysis of past Fed leadership transitions reveals a consistent pattern: yields have typically climbed in the three months following a new chair’s appointment. On average, the two-year Treasury yield (US2Y) (UTWO) (XTWO) has climbed 55 basis points, while the 10-year (US10Y) (IEF) (VGIT) has increased 48 bps during these transition periods.

The most dramatic repricing occurred under Paul Volcker, when the two-year yield (US2Y) surged 288 bps and the 10-year (US10Y) jumped 194 bps as markets rapidly adjusted to his aggressive anti-inflation stance.

The pattern isn’t universal, though. Yields fell after Arthur Burns took over in 1970, and the response under Alan Greenspan was relatively muted.

data.title: Uber Eats will now return customer orders made at participating retailers
data.description:
• Uber Eats (UBER) will now pick up and return packages for customers to eligible retailers as part of a new service announced on Friday.
  • The service will apply to orders of at least $20 made on Uber Eats (UBER) for retailers on the platform, including Best Buy (BBY), Dick’s Sporting Goods (DKS), Lowe’s (LOW) and Target (TGT), among others.
  • Refunds for the order will be applied when the courier picks up the item.
  • Return a Package is a flat $5 fee, or $3 for Uber One members.
  • The new service differs from its Uber Courier service, which will pick up prepaid and sealed packages and drop them off at the USPS, UPS (UPS), or FedEx (FDX) office for delivery.

data.title: Boeing CEO says Iran conflict hasn’t disrupted jet deliveries, sees defense upside
data.description:
Boeing Co. (BA) management said ongoing conflict involving Iran hasn't affected its commercial aircraft deliveries, while the company expects heightened geopolitical tensions to support growth in its defense operations.

Chief Executive Kelly Ortberg, speaking Friday at Boeing’s (BA) annual shareholder meeting, said airline customers are still accepting new aircraft on schedule despite concerns that regional instability could ripple through global aviation markets.

Ortberg also struck an upbeat tone on Boeing’s (BA) narrowbody manufacturing plans, saying the company remains on track to raise monthly production of the 737 Max to 47 aircraft this year from the current rate of 42.

The comments suggest Boeing continues to regain momentum in its commercial business as it works to stabilize factory output and improve delivery performance after years of operational setbacks.

At the same time, Ortberg said Boeing’s (BA) defense unit could benefit from the conflict, pointing to potential increases in maintenance work, service contracts and demand for munitions.

The remarks come as aerospace and defense companies monitor whether rising military tensions translate into larger government spending commitments and faster procurement cycles. For Boeing, that could provide an additional tailwind as it seeks stronger performance across both its commercial and defense businesses.
